children.name: military and service
So when things final cooled down one guy—American, our troops—he was going out just to be a listening post at the edge of the wood line, because we had our dead troops there.
So when it finally cooled down they was not firing back at us, and we assumed that either they were gone or they were dead, the enemy.
They don’t have this kind of information in books.
We never had any violence because we did not cross the line.
And they have very little in the books about the Vietnam War.
This company had got hit pretty hard by the enemy and they didn’t have really enough to maintain so they rebuilt the fourth by adding more troops out there.
So we had our helicopters call in and we had to load up our dead.
We always have gotten our dead out; not under fire.
You fight the Vietcong which can use booby traps and all that kind of stuff.
We didn’t have to worry about booby traps, pungent pits, and North Vietnamese regiments.
But anyway they had killed these Vietnamese, and normally that we don’t bury the enemy, but it was an area that the United States used; a convoy going through.
And the dead was there for about a week.

And sometimes when we would go out on AOP, which is a listening post, we might go out that night like phone in, go out from the main compound in case the enemy was trying to move at night, but we never did experienced the enemy moving at night.
And then later on they brought some ammunition in and it kinda cooled down then.
I was just praying to myself that I hope all the enemy down there is dead and nobody’s gonna shoot me while—which they were dead, dead or either they had backed off.
The country today can be argued is much different in terms of how people look at, and respond to social issues than they did in the late 1960s.
The late 1960s there was the Vietnam War, there was the movement, there was King, there was Johnson not running for election.
And I ask that because many people I speak to from your generation tell me that something is missing today that used to exist.
And I understand that you’re talking about segregation, but I was trying to get it spoken to in your own words.
That’s talking about in the [19]50s.
protest on social issues where that doesn’t happen today.
There’s the internet, people are satisfied with petitions and just sit and watch, and music—just like it was before, but a little bit different.
The last time that we spoke you were very forthcoming in your discussion of your participation in the Vietnam War, and also your experiences with the Veteran’s Administration following.
So I went and I talked, and I guess I don’t want to tell this story before I get to talking about Vietnam unless you want me to talk into it now, but I had to tell them how I lived in Vietnam.

W:	Alright we’re returning to our interview with Mr. Cornelius Clayton on September 9th, 2011 after a brief interlude, and we are ready to move from Vietnam, and talk about what happened when you got home; your career opportunities and your continuing education.
But I was able, and I will continue as long as I’m able if somebody ask me to speak, and tell what went down, because a lot of time what you see on TV is not what happened over there.
I’ll leave it to you to tell us what happened when you got back.
family and home
W:	Okay now about your parent’s parents, if we could take it back a generation, who were your mother’s parents?
C:	Okay I didn’t have the opportunity of meeting my dad’s dad, but my grandfather which was my mother’s father I was raised up around him.
C:	They all deceased, but my dad was Cornelius Clayton Sr. and my mother was Ruby Lee Clayton which is deceased.
W:	Do you have any further memories of the parents of your grandparents or any other information that you could share about the extended family?
[Laughter] I didn’t really wanna know about parents, family and marriage.
W:	Who were your parents Mr. Clayton?
Do you recall your parents’ birthdays?
W:	And your dad?
C:	I can’t remember my dad’s.
C:	Okay, Maddie Magali was my mother’s mother, which was my grandmother.
I don’t remember my father’s mother.
W:	And how about the grandfathers on each side?
Your dad’s dad and your mom’s dad?
W:	Your grandparent’s parents on each side?
C:	I got married 1992 my present wife.
Because we got married right out of high school.
W:	Was there a large period of time between the end of your first marriage and the start of your second marriage, or did you get married again right away?
C:	My mother was December the 7th, 1922.
Okay, are you married?
I am married.
And what is your wife’s name?
I have three kids—one by first marriage; Cassandra Clayton and Cornelius
and one offspring by ex-girlfriend that’s thirty-three.
First wife or second wife?
Well the first wife was in high school during the high school time.
We sit around, and we talk and we laugh, and I’d take pictures, and I’d video tape it.
Some sitting in the seat, and some are sitting around the floor.
We could go to the kitchen inside and order our hamburger and take it home, or take it wherever and eat it.
As for them I don’t really care for the chicken and so we have a good time.
I think you said
They like to do the bonfire and sit around.
We have a good time.
clothes out and some hot chow.
Frederick Clayton, which he is in the military; my daughter lives in Gainesville;
My brother lives out of Atlanta, and my sister resides in Gainesville.
She lives in Orlando.
Mr. Clayton, would you please give your full name?
Evangeline Grimes and my brother’s name is Ernest Vincent Clayton.
Her name is Thelma Clayton.
Her name is Detra Clayton.
career and work and money
W:	How long did he operate his business?
W:	Everybody had problems with Lincoln.
But he owned his own business and he did well.
C:	He operated his business.
C:	Lincoln had some big boys, too.
C:	His name was Jimmy Cooper.
C:	His name was DC.
My uncle, Ernest Magali Jr. had already learned the well drilling business, so he carried the business on.
And my uncle—he learned those trades from him about mechanics when he was coming up as a teenager.
Except my grandfather, he was a well driller and he owned his own business, that’s different from the stores.
grandfather was a great mechanic.
Also my grandfather back in the [19]20s, we had a—that was before my time—a car dealership, which was selling model marlay malty, whatever.
And my grandfather he was the mechanic.
